content.claim: Cross-national econometric studies (Hanushek & Woessmann) using difference-in-differences design on TIMSS/PIRLS/PISA data find that early tracking increases educational inequality and shows a tendency (though less clear) to reduce mean performance.
content.context: Fresh live research through the International comparison lens
limitations: Explicitly contested: large replication (75 countries, 20 years) found tracking-inequality effects inconclusive and dependent on domain and inequality measure
Country-level analysis with small sample sizes limits causal inference validity
Does not distinguish between age-10 tracking (Hungary, Germany, Austria) and later tracking
content.population: Population described in the cited finding
content.time_scope: As reported by the cited source
